turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Number 573, National Labor Relations Board, petitioner, versus Gissel Packing Co., Inc., et al. and Number 691, Food Store Employees Union, Local No. 347, Amalgamated Meat Cutters & Butchers Workmen of North America, versus Gissel Packing Company. Mr. Manoli.

turns.name: DOMINICK L. MANOLI
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice and may it please the Court. These cases number 573 and 691 are here on writs of certiorari to the Court of Appeals for the Fourth Circuit. The Labor Act requires an employer to bargain with their union or with their representative which in the words of the statute has been designated or selected by a majority of the employees in an appropriate collective bargaining union. For more than 30 years, this Court and the Courts of Appeals both under the original Act as well as the present Act have enforced Board orders which required an employer to bargain with the Union which the employees had designated as their bargain representative by means of authorization cards and the employer did not have a “good faith” doubt of the Union's majority status. The court below, the court below which in the past had accepted authorization's card as adequate proof of a union's majority status for purposes of a bargaining of the bargaining obligation under Section 8 (a) (5) of the statute now holds that these authorization cards are inherently unreliable and that an employer, when he is confronted with the demand for recognition by union, whose representative status is base solely upon these authorization cards. That the employer may assert a good faith doubt of the Union's majority status, refused to bargain, insist upon an election and all of these irrespective of the fact that concurrently with his refusal to bargain with -- to recognize and bargain with the Union as the majority representative concurrently with that action. He is engaging in serious unfair labor practice which may either belie his reliance upon his good faith doubt or preclude a fair election. Hence, the central question in these cases and not only these two, these cases that were shared from the Court up here and from the Court of Appeals but also in Sinclair case which from the First Circuit which will follow these cases. The central question in these cases is whether the Board may require an employer to bargain with the Union whose representative status is based solely upon authorization cards from a majority of the employees in the particular unit. And the employer's concurrent misconduct at the time that he refuses to bargain with the Union on the base of those cards is the concurrent misconduct demonstrates, demonstrates that is asserted doubt -- that he is refusing, he is refusing to bargain with the Union not because of any asserted doubt of the Union's majority status but in order to gain time, in order to dissipate the Union's strength and avoid bargaining at all. Now, would obviously --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Is there a question about the -- about whether a subsequent election changes any of this?

turns.name: DOMINICK L. MANOLI
turns.text: The --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Do we have to decide that question?

turns.name: DOMINICK L. MANOLI
turns.text: The Boards -- I think you're getting at what the Board calls it burnout from rule. In other words, if a union comes to an employer, goes to an employer, and demands recognition on the basis of cards, and the employer engages an unfair labor practice at that particular time. The Board will nevertheless permit the Union to go to an election. If the Union loses that election because of the unfair labor practice committed by the employer during the pre-election period, the Board will permit the Union then to go ahead an unfair labor practice charge and on the basis of the cards demonstrate that it had a majority and that the employer --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Do we have to decide that in this case?

turns.name: DOMINICK L. MANOLI
turns.text: The --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: In these cases --

turns.name: DOMINICK L. MANOLI
turns.text: -- that problem -- you don't have to decide that in the Fourth Circuit case.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: No.

turns.name: DOMINICK L. MANOLI
turns.text: But that issue is present however in the Sinclair case, the one from the First Circuit because all, excuse me for one moment, I'll take that back. In one of the cases here -- in one of the case here, there was an election which the Union --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: So we do that to decide that?

turns.name: DOMINICK L. MANOLI
turns.text: -- and that question is here. That's right. And it is also in the Sinclair case as I recall it. Now obviously the time that I have at my disposal, I will not be able to go into the details of these cases here but I think I can fairly say -- I can fairly say that the factual framework in which this question arises in all three cases is not essentially dissimilar. And in each case, you had the Union which engage in an organizing campaign among the employees and in the course of that organizing campaign it obtain cards authorizing it, unqualifiedly. There's no question about the cards. There's no ambiguity about these cards here which are reproduced in our brief, which unqualifiedly designated the Union as their bargain representatives -- as their bargain representatives and only one of these cases from the Fourth Circuit. I believe that social to the Sinclair although I don't want to get into that one. In only one of these cases was any question raised as to the validity of the cards in the General Steel case that claim was made that these cards were obtained by mis -- by virtue of misrepresentations as to their purposes. The Board on the basis of the entire record upon the basis of the evidence before rejected that claim and found that all these cards here, all of these cards here unqualifiedly designated the Union as the bargain representative and that there was no impropriety in the procurement of these cards. Now armed with these cards, the Union went to each of the three employers in these particular cases here and asks for recognition. The employer questioning the validity of the cards, question the Union's majority status and refuse to bargain to the Union insisted upon an election.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: On what grounds did they question?

turns.name: DOMINICK L. MANOLI
turns.text: Oh, I think that it varied from case to case.And some of them the employers was said -- in effect said, we don't -- we don't rely on cards. We think that they are unreliable because the circumstances and I wonder which they may be may have been procured. In another case an employer said, I don't think I should recognize you because four years ago, four years ago -- there was an election in which this particular union had participated and got lost. And still another case that employer simply said, no comment. No comment and still another case the employer when asked to recognizing on the basis of the cards said that we think you ought to go to an election. And I think there's one case here where the employer really never questions the cards, never question cards and his answer was, “no comment”. No comment and there seems to be some evidence in the record here that the employer actually knew that a majority of employers had selected that particular --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Well, Mr. Manoli, what is the Board's view of what the employer must, and like they say, what is there position that he must take before the Board that will recognize that he may insist on an election rather than to a Court recognition based on cards?

turns.name: DOMINICK L. MANOLI
turns.text: Your Honor, if the Union goes to an employer and says to the employer, we have cards here from the majority of your employees which as their bargain representative. The employer may say, the Board has said this -- the employer may say, “I don't like cards. I don't wish to rely upon your cards and I do insist that you go to an election.” I think that that's the proper way to resolve this question this representation question is to go to an election. If he does no more than that, he may even engage. I mean during the pre-election period, he may engage in the speech -- he make speeches to his employees --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: But that -- but that won't that may not get him -- the Union may just file 8 (b) – 8 (a) (5) but charges on them in.

turns.name: DOMINICK L. MANOLI
turns.text: The Board will -- the general counsel will not issue a complaint on the basis of that in that kind of situation where the employer says for the Union, I don't wish to rely upon cards --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: I don't care how many cards you've got. I just don't like it.

turns.name: DOMINICK L. MANOLI
turns.text: I don't care how many -- that's right. I don't care how many cards you got --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: He can get an election then?

turns.name: DOMINICK L. MANOLI
turns.text: That's right. As long as --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: That's your rep – that's represents what the position of the Board is --

turns.name: DOMINICK L. MANOLI
turns.text: That's right. As long as he does not misbehave, now that's why they say.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: As long as what?

turns.name: DOMINICK L. MANOLI
turns.text: He does not misbehave, does not engage in contemporaneously with the refusal to accept those cards that he does not engage in unfair labor practice and serious unfair labor practice because the Board in some case where the employer may have engaged in some isolated unfair labor practice. It still will permit it to refuse to bargain with the Union. It's only when it engages in serious unfair labor practice --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Its certainly doesn't emerge from your briefs in this case?

turns.name: DOMINICK L. MANOLI
turns.text: I hope it didn't.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Has this been -- has this always been the practice Mr. Manoli?

turns.name: DOMINICK L. MANOLI
turns.text: No, Your Honor. I think the Board is change a little bit --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Well, I may explain in times since I've been at it but the --

turns.name: DOMINICK L. MANOLI
turns.text: -- the -- with the Board has change a little bit in his approach to these cards here. I think that one --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Quite a bit. Yes.

turns.name: DOMINICK L. MANOLI
turns.text: But its not been a recent change really, Your Honor. I think that this began back sometime in the early 60's. At one time, the Board would take the position and simply I might say that we sold virtually all the courts, all the courts with this proposition that an employer when a union came to him and said, I -- we want to be recognized based on these cards, it was not enough for him to say, I don't like cards. He had to have or not enough for him to say that I have a good faith doubt of your majority status. He also had to have some objective evidence which supported his good faith doubt. Today, the Board does not insist that when an employer is confronted with a demand for recognition --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Where is this articulated in the Board opinion somewhere its due?

turns.name: DOMINICK L. MANOLI
turns.text: The -- well, I think you have to take the Aaron Brothers case, Your Honor. The Aaron Brothers case which is cited in our brief and that one here has made it very clear. And also, you might take a look at page 22 of our brief where we indicate its not in every case even if -- even where the employer may commit some unfair labor for practices, excuse me I'm getting away from this thing. Even where the employer may commit some unfair labor practice when he refuses to accept these cards --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Well -- well if the key then Mr. Manoli that he has no right or rather the Board will not recognize that he should not be concluded by cards except in situations where there some kind of unfair labor practice that is connected with his refusal to --

turns.name: DOMINICK L. MANOLI
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: -- the Court recognition based on current --

turns.name: DOMINICK L. MANOLI
turns.text: That's right. Words contemporaneous, contemporaneous with refusal upon --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: And even -- and even then his right to insists on election is lost only, if I understood you, if his unfair labor practices of -- are of a serious nature?

turns.name: DOMINICK L. MANOLI
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: And is that what this case is all about?

turns.name: DOMINICK L. MANOLI
turns.text: That's what we think is the narrow issue as we see that's presented in this particular case because I didn't get through with my facts.

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: Well, Mr. Manoli, --

turns.name: DOMINICK L. MANOLI
turns.text: But I think I can skip those from --

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: Alright. Mr. Manoli, this so I could be sure that this into the bench, I heard you correctly.

turns.name: DOMINICK L. MANOLI
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: The cards are obtained by the Union.

turns.name: DOMINICK L. MANOLI
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: The employer says, I won't recognize these cards, and he petitions for an election. No acts of -- no unfair labor practices intervene. Now, what happens between the time that the Union submit its cards and the time the election is held. Does the employer have to bargain with the Union then --

turns.name: DOMINICK L. MANOLI
turns.text: No, sir.

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: -- or does he not?

turns.name: DOMINICK L. MANOLI
turns.text: No, sir.

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: And you're saying, he does not have to bargain.

turns.name: DOMINICK L. MANOLI
turns.text: No, sir. He does not have bargain.

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: I didn't get that either.

turns.name: DOMINICK L. MANOLI
turns.text: He can wait for the election. And again, I want to emphasize Your Honor that of course this is the case in which has been no misbehavior that kind that I'm speaking about in the --

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: Well, I said that. I said that --

turns.name: DOMINICK L. MANOLI
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: But I didn't understand that from your brief either?

turns.name: DOMINICK L. MANOLI
turns.text: The -- no, there's no obligation of bargain. If the Union confronts with the cards, under the Board's view of the law, the employer can say, I want to go to an election until that election --

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Did in anytime he ask for as long as the designated one say, I want --

turns.name: DOMINICK L. MANOLI
turns.text: The one election that he doesn't wish to rely upon cards.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Supplemental to what is that. Assuming the employer here so that they can't (Inaudible) authorization letter.

turns.name: DOMINICK L. MANOLI
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Does the employer have to same right in that context (Voice Overlap)?

turns.name: DOMINICK L. MANOLI
turns.text: Yes, he does. He does and there is --

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Same right or free speech?

turns.name: DOMINICK L. MANOLI
turns.text: He has the same right or his speech.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Say this is much but no more than (Inaudible).

turns.name: DOMINICK L. MANOLI
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: And that's the Board's position?

turns.name: DOMINICK L. MANOLI
turns.text: That's the Board's position. Now, of course what this free speech -- what isn't free speech -- you will --

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: What is that?

turns.name: DOMINICK L. MANOLI
turns.text: -- you will hear more of that as in the Sinclair case. But he is the Board will permit him to speak his speech to the employers who are trying to dissuade them not to vote for the Union, not to support for the Union --

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Or not to sign out authorization card.

turns.name: DOMINICK L. MANOLI
turns.text: Or not to sign out authorization card, that's right. He can do that.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: I don't like you keep repeating myself --

turns.name: DOMINICK L. MANOLI
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: -- with this -- at least I think (Inaudible). Now, I correctly understand the Board's position to lead that the only time that an employer is not bound with the Court's decision based upon cards and the other situations of private election in that situation which you (Inaudible) of serious unfair labor practice.

turns.name: DOMINICK L. MANOLI
turns.text: That's correct. With one – may I just add a very small fact on to that kind of case? It really doesn't -- but in order to be completely accurately. The Board had a case in which the employer when the Union demand recognition on the basis of the cards. He did not question the cards. He did not raise any question about this majority but he refused to bargain with the Union because he says he thought. He thought that he was not subject to the Labor Act. He was not subject to Labor Act. Subsequently, as an afterthought you might say, he said well, I don't want to recognize you because I don't rely on cards. That's really an off beat kind of a case. In that particular case, the Board would not permit the employer to come up -- after having refuse to bargain with the Union on the basis of this that he did not come under the Act would not permit him to resurrect or inject into the picture, his good faith doubt of the Union's majority status. But as I say that's a kind of an off beat type of case but your statement what I -- Board's position today is correct Mr. Justice Brennan.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: But the employer -- that the employer might say, I don't believe in cards and I don't think you guys you need to certify. And then he just makes it --

turns.name: DOMINICK L. MANOLI
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: -- and then -- then the Union will ask him about --

turns.name: DOMINICK L. MANOLI
turns.text: Right. Either he or the Union under the statute may ask for an election.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: (Inaudible) apparently not to be those committed before the Union asked him to recognize that it would be unfair labor practices afterwards?

turns.name: DOMINICK L. MANOLI
turns.text: No. There are unfair labor practices that may take during the course of the Union organizing campaign.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: But even that they did. That the first they ever heard to your campaign was (Inaudible)?

turns.name: DOMINICK L. MANOLI
turns.text: Yes. And what's equalizing --

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: And he said, “I want an election.”

turns.name: DOMINICK L. MANOLI
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: And then he goes to commit some unfair labor practice by the same rule?

turns.name: DOMINICK L. MANOLI
turns.text: The same rule applies. If he commits so serious unfair labor practice then that the Union has a majority of the cards which are otherwise valid. He will be subject to a bargain Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: Well, is there any magic in what words he says when he rejects him?

turns.name: DOMINICK L. MANOLI
turns.text: No, I don't think so Your Honor, but the Board has never had the case in which the employer just said when the Union asked him to a -- to bargain and to recognize it and say, “Go away”, and said no more than that or said, “To go away and I want an election.” Usually of course, with your case where the employer will say, “I don't trust cards. I don't think they are reliable. I think we have to go an election.” But although the Board is not dealt with the first case, I don't think that any meaningful distinction can be drawn between two cases.

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: Well in these cases here, there's nothing on the language that was used to reject in the cause.

turns.name: DOMINICK L. MANOLI
turns.text: Sir?

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: There is nothing significant about the language in these cases that was used in rejecting the cause, is that right?

turns.name: DOMINICK L. MANOLI
turns.text: Well, I think you will find without spelling out which particular case in which the employer was suppose to or so as he did say. We don't want to rely upon cards. We want to go to an election.

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: DOMINICK L. MANOLI
turns.text: -- and in another I think there were two of those cases. There were -- another -- one of these cases here where the Union actually put that authorization cards in front of the employer. The employer took a glance out and then he says. He said, “I don't want to go on the basis of cards. We want to go on election.” So as I say, I wouldn't draw any narrow distinction between where the employer may not be articulate enough to say, “Well, look I've heard all the story, all the stories about cards not being very reliable.” But if he simply says, “I want to go to an election. I don't -- I just -- that's I think the way union should establish his majority status is by election.” Then I think that the Board will permit it. The Board will permit him to refuse to bargain without union to recognize it --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Or even if he said, “don't waste my time, go to an election.”

turns.name: DOMINICK L. MANOLI
turns.text: Yes, I think so. I think so. The -- well, I will not -- I think we've pretty much got in the facts of these of cases out, so I won't spend any more time with that. I've already indicated what the Fourth Circuit's opinion is as the unreliability of these cards. Now, I want to go into the two arguments that I think ought to be brought out in connection with these cads. The -- our analysis of course begins with Section 85 of the Act. Section 85 of the Act makes unfair labor practice for an employer to refuse to bargain with the representative of his employees as defined in Sections 9 (a) of the Act. Section 9 (a) of the Act in turn defines a representative as one which has been designated or selected by a majority of the employees in an appropriate bargaining unit. Section 9 (a) does not specify, it does not specify how the employees will designate or select their employees and both under the original Wagner Act as well as the present Act. The Courts of Appeals had consistently recognize, had consistently recognize that a union could establish its majority status not only by means of an election but also by other means including, including authorization cards. And this Court in the Arkansas Flooring case which was decided in 19 -- of some nine years, some nine years after they Taft-Hartley amendments of 1947 and I'll come to those amendments in just a moment. The Court in that particular case said that a union may establish its selection that an election was not the only means for a union to establish its representative status but that it could do so by other means including authorization cards. Now, the legislative history of -- the 1947 amendments on which the court below heavily rely for its contrary view -- for its contrary review, we submit, we suggest that it does not bar. It does not bar reliance upon authorization cards as proof of the Union's majority status. I might say that the court below is the only one that the Court of Appeals and frankly, all the Courts of Appeals have had this problem that the law -- that the Court below is the only one that interprets the 94 -- 1947 amendments to bar, to bar the use cards for the purpose of establishing a union's majority status as a predicate for bargaining order under Section 85 and it's the only Court that has set that only that the Union must establish his majority status -- can only establish his majority status as a predicate for bargaining or only via the election root.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: May I ask you Mr. Manoli just --

turns.name: DOMINICK L. MANOLI
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: -- out of ignorance. Prior to 1947, prior to Taft-Harley Act, there was no provision for an election at the request of the employer.

turns.name: DOMINICK L. MANOLI
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Am I wrong about that?

turns.name: DOMINICK L. MANOLI
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Or right about that?

turns.name: DOMINICK L. MANOLI
turns.text: Yes, that's quite right.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: But there -- was there in the old Wagner -- in the Wagner Act up until 1947 provision for Board supervisor elections at the request of the Union?

turns.name: DOMINICK L. MANOLI
turns.text: Under the Wagner Act, the Wagner Act made no provision as Your Honors indicated for an employer to file a representation petition. It was the Board's practice in those days to entertain a petition from an employer if two or more unions --

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: DOMINICK L. MANOLI
turns.text: -- if two or more unions were make -- demanded recognition. In that case, the Board would entertain -- would entertain the election, the petition --

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Although, there was no explicit statutory authority?

turns.name: DOMINICK L. MANOLI
turns.text: Sir?

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Although, there was no explicit statutory authority?

turns.name: DOMINICK L. MANOLI
turns.text: No explicit. It made no provision.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: DOMINICK L. MANOLI
turns.text: It was completely sudden, it made no provision the 47 amendments did take care of that particular problem because under the 47 amendments an employer is now permitted to file his representation petition whether its one or more unions -- one or more unions that --

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: The predicate for that however still you have -- it has to be a demand on the employer by a union --

turns.name: DOMINICK L. MANOLI
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: -- representative --

turns.name: DOMINICK L. MANOLI
turns.text: But that's right.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: -- at least one union.

turns.name: DOMINICK L. MANOLI
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: And prior to 1947, was there explicit statutory provision for Board supervised elections at the request of a union?

turns.name: DOMINICK L. MANOLI
turns.text: The -- under the Wagner Act, there was a provision in Section 9 there which empowered the Board to ascertain whenever a question concerning representation arose to determine that question either by means of an election or partly in the exact language of the Wagner Act or by other suitable means, other suitable means for ascertaining the employee's wishes. Again, the 47 Amendment knocked that part of the Act. By other suitable means and the 47 amendments made up board election the sole basis for a formal board certification. Of course, a board bargaining order is to be distinguished from the Board certification which is use following --

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: What was the practice of these prior 1947, were there many elections?

turns.name: DOMINICK L. MANOLI
turns.text: Yes, there were. There were elections. I thought Your Honor was going to asked me, did the Board certify unions since all the suitable methods were also available to it? Did it often certify union on the basis of something other than election?

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Well, that was -- you framed my question better but that's what in my mind.

turns.name: DOMINICK L. MANOLI
turns.text: The Board did that until 1939. It would certify union on the basis of cards or some other. Well, presume about, usually cards or maybe some other written proof of this majority status. In 1939, the Board abandoned that practice but the Board however continued to certify a union on the basis of cards while the employer and the Union, both agreed to have a crossed check of the cards as against the payroll in order to determine the authenticity of the cards. And in that particular case -- in that particular situation, the Board continued after 1939 not many of those cases, I suspect, I really don't know how many, but probably not many but nonetheless the Board would continue to issue certifications on the basis of that kind of a crossed check with both parties agreed that if the crossed check was correct, the certification would issue. Again, if I may come back quickly to the 1947 amendments, the 47 amendments do no longer permit the Board certification to issue on anything but in an election. The --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Mr. Manoli, isn't that the --

turns.name: DOMINICK L. MANOLI
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: But assume the Union's representative come to the employer and presents his cards (Inaudible) it says, well certainly it appears that you have a majority and I have no question about these cards and you still like you've got a majority of the workers with you but I want an election.

turns.name: DOMINICK L. MANOLI
turns.text: The Board will permit him to go on election as long as he does not misbehave.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Well, the test you state in your brief --

turns.name: DOMINICK L. MANOLI
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: -- is whether or not the employer has a good faith doubt about the majority status of the Union and that unfair labor practice is only a material element in judging whether or not he had a good faith doubt. Now, you're stating --

turns.name: DOMINICK L. MANOLI
turns.text: Sir --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: -- a considerably different rule in that.

turns.name: DOMINICK L. MANOLI
turns.text: If Your Honor -- if Your Honors --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Don't you think the rule you're stating is different?

turns.name: DOMINICK L. MANOLI
turns.text: No, I think it's the rule we've indicated -- it's the Board rule in our process --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: You mean that -- yes, but the employer says, I have no doubt at all about your majority status. No good faith -- I have no good faith doubt --

turns.name: DOMINICK L. MANOLI
turns.text: Let me --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: I'm -- but I just want an election.

turns.name: DOMINICK L. MANOLI
turns.text: The -- I think that under the Board -- let me put two different kind of cases in order to answer Your Honor's question.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Well, how about --

turns.name: DOMINICK L. MANOLI
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Just that --let me -- the employer does no more -- and he says. He says explicit -- I have no good faith doubt that you have majority there but I'm not going to recognize you without an election. I want an election.

turns.name: DOMINICK L. MANOLI
turns.text: But is he saying, if I may put to your question in return. Is he saying that I have no doubt -- I have no doubt that you have cards here signed by a majority --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: I have no good faith doubt that you do.

turns.name: DOMINICK L. MANOLI
turns.text: -- or I have no good faith doubt that these cards have been signed by majority of my employees but I still don't like cards and I want to go an election.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Well, he doesn't say, --

turns.name: DOMINICK L. MANOLI
turns.text: The board --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: --“I don't like cards.” He says, “I want an election.”

turns.name: DOMINICK L. MANOLI
turns.text: I think in that kind of a case, the Board will permit him to go on election. Now, let me put this case --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Will permit him -- will permit him.

turns.name: DOMINICK L. MANOLI
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: And they won't -- and they will not have met then say that he's been guilty of an 8 (a) (5) --

turns.name: DOMINICK L. MANOLI
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: -- violation.

turns.name: DOMINICK L. MANOLI
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Now, that seems to me considerably different test that you got in your brief but you honestly accept it though.

turns.name: DOMINICK L. MANOLI
turns.text: Let me put this case, --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Alright. He wants to accept this test.

turns.name: DOMINICK L. MANOLI
turns.text: Yes. That's right. Now, let me put this case. Now, suppose that the employer when faced with these cards he said -- he would say, “I've made my own independent check of the employees and I discovered from my own independent check of the employees and independently of your cards that they do want to use their bargain representative.” Now, that case there, he will be required to bargain with the Union even though he'd be not engaged in any unfair labor practice. I want to make that clear. Even the Fourth Circuit in that kind of a case would enforce a bargaining order where the employer has made its own independent poll of the employees and on the basis of that he's been -- he -- the employees have told him, “Yes, we want this union a majority of them. Then even Fourth Circuit.” The Fourth Circuit will --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: You mean if he has a mass meeting of the employees said, “Do you fellows really want this union?” And unanimously they say, “We sure do.”

turns.name: DOMINICK L. MANOLI
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: And he has to recognize.

turns.name: DOMINICK L. MANOLI
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: You draw up --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Getting in there?

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: You draw the line then between in saying that I have no doubt -- good faith doubt that these are the signatures. And he is saying that I have no doubt that you do represent the majority. You draw a line between those two.

turns.name: DOMINICK L. MANOLI
turns.text: If he says, “Yes, I think you would draw a line between those two and I don't --

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: If he says, I have no doubt that these are the signatures of 51% of my employees and I have no doubt that 51% of the employees want you but I still want an election.”

turns.name: DOMINICK L. MANOLI
turns.text: If you put that way, it cannot insist upon an election when --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: You say, he can or can not?

turns.name: DOMINICK L. MANOLI
turns.text: He can not. He can not insist upon election because there -- there he's not merely saying in the case of Mr. Justice Marshall has put to us. He's not merely saying, I know you got cards here, but he's also saying that he knows. He knows independently of the cards, independent of the cards of the majority of the employees have designated that union.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: But it is only his evidence is the -- of his -- whether he knows or not is the cards.

turns.name: DOMINICK L. MANOLI
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: I think he'd have the election.

turns.name: DOMINICK L. MANOLI
turns.text: Yes, that's right. Now, to go on with the legislative history that as I've indicated that the courts had uniformly -- had uniformly interpreted both the Wagner Act and Taft-Hartley Act to permit the use of cards for union to demonstrate its majority as majority status. The 47 amendments did not change either Section 8 (a) (5) or which was 8 (5) in the original Wagner Act or Section 9 (a). But in fact, the legislative history the 1947 amendments shows that the conference committee specifically rejected a house passed amendments which have -- where have relieve the employer, which we have a relieve the employer of any obligation to bargain with the Union, which based its majority status upon cards. Congress specifically rejected that proposal which would have amended Section 8 (a) (5) so as to permit the Board to issue a bargaining order only when the employer was either refusing to bargain with the Union that he was currently recognizing or a union which have been certified by the Board after an election. Congress says that it rejected that amendment, rejected that amendment and retained the language of Section 8 (a) (5), Sections 9 (a) which from the very beginning of this statute had been interpreted both by the Board, by the courts as permitting the use of cards to establish a union as majority status. The 1959 amendments when there was discussion in -- when there is the legislative consideration of the 1959 amendments, Congress again took note of the fact that a Board -- that a bargaining obligation under Section 8 (5), that the issuance of a Board bargaining order was not dependent upon a board election. It took specific note of Board cases where the Board had required an employer to bargain with the Union which was representative status was based upon cards and where the employer had engaged in misconduct which belies his good faith doubt. Nonetheless, the 1959 Congress did not amend the basic language of Section 8 (5) or Section 9 which as I repeatedly offset is the basis for the use of cards as a means for establishing union's majority position. Now, its argued -- as argued very strongly that cards because of the circumstances -- because of the circumstances that may attend their procurement are inherently unreliable and that an election is the only proper way of safeguarding the employees and permitting them to vote their convictions. Alright, I would think that the fact that the Congress -- that Congress must have discounted this argument when in 1947, it rejected the House passed Amendment which was where relieved the employer of any obligation to bargain with the Union whose representative status was based upon cards.

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: What was the Board's practice at that time -- at the time that Congress rejected that? What was the Board's practice with respect to cards?

turns.name: DOMINICK L. MANOLI
turns.text: The Boards was been using cards all along every since the beginning --

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: I understand but there's been some shift in the certainties and the --

turns.name: DOMINICK L. MANOLI
turns.text: Yes, sir. Has been --

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: -- new onsets and the --

turns.name: DOMINICK L. MANOLI
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: -- grace notes of all these?

turns.name: DOMINICK L. MANOLI
turns.text: This shift has been I think primarily in this respect that the Board originally would say the employer had to come forward with some objective evidence to support his good faith doubt. Today the Board has not insists that he come forward with the objective evidence. In fact, the Board puts it burden of proving the employer's lack of a good faith doubt -- puts that burden upon the general counsel.

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: What you're saying is -- are you telling me then that in 1947 --

turns.name: DOMINICK L. MANOLI
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: -- and in 1959 when Congress, according to you, rejected in fact on the use of cards, the Board's practice was at those times was to accept the cards, require that the employer bargain on the basis of cards unless there was an affirmative showing of bad faith?

turns.name: DOMINICK L. MANOLI
turns.text: Unless -- unless he had a good faith doubt based upon objective considerations. I would like phrase it that way.

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: Unless he had a good faith doubt based on objective consideration -- I withdraw bad faith.

turns.name: DOMINICK L. MANOLI
turns.text: All right, sir. The -- I'm afraid my time is up. I have to leave the rest of it to the brief. This is a case that might have needed a little bit more time.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Very well.

turns.name: DOMINICK L. MANOLI
turns.text: Thank you very much.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Mr. Gore.

turns.name: ALBERT GORE
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, may it please the Court. I'm in the anomalous position of having to come to this Court to argue a case in conjunction with the Board with some slight differences and now in the position of having to take the position almost diametrical to that which has been argued today. The statement of Board counsel in respect to the responses to the respective questions of Your Honors, do not in my view suggest the rule of that the Board is now following nor do they suggest the rule that the law lays doubt. The inherent interpretation that can be predicated upon counsel's responses to the questions is that cards are unreliable because he argues as we do that there are two ways to gain recognition. There are two ways to impose a duty to recognize upon an employer, i.e. the root by way of the election, and second, the root by way of authorization cards, unambiguous, unequivocal, signed by a majority of the employees without restrain or coercion. It was this rule that was approved by Congress of 1947. It is this rule which has been approved that by Congress in 1959. It's this rule which Congress, there are certain members of Congress has -- have attempted to change unsuccessfully. The rule as we see it is that when as in Gissel Packing the case in which we are the petitioner. A majority of the employees signed clear and unambiguous cards saying, “I authorize this union to represent me in wages or wages hours and conditions of employment.” And when this majority of cards are tendered to the employer, in one form or another and in the instant case, they were tendered to the employer himself not to third party. And they say, “Here are the cards. We represent the majority.” At that point as we view the law, the employer is under a duty to recognize.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: When -- when they say, no, I want an election, under your view?

turns.name: ALBERT GORE
turns.text: Your Honor, he may not say no. I want an election. What he may do under the inherent construction of the Act is to file for an election himself and then if he engages in no conduct which is inconsistent with the free election and election can be had.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: You mean, he can -- may not insist upon the Union secret election --

turns.name: ALBERT GORE
turns.text: That is correct.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: But he may himself say, I won't now write the Board for an election.

turns.name: ALBERT GORE
turns.text: That is correct because --

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: That's -- that sort of a --

turns.name: ALBERT GORE
turns.text: Well, Your Honor --

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: -- through the Government's little deed?

turns.name: ALBERT GORE
turns.text: Well, no it is not.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Uh-huh.

turns.name: ALBERT GORE
turns.text: It is not because there is in labor relations, the tremendous important factor of time --

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Well, the employer though wouldn't necessarily give the election?

turns.name: ALBERT GORE
turns.text: Oh, yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Why would he give it?

turns.name: ALBERT GORE
turns.text: If the employer files a petition because the one Amendment in 1947 that has some relevance to this case. Was the amendment in Section 9 (c) (1) (d) and the way this came up was that Congress was concerned with the fact that a union may come to an employer and demand recognition, and the employer was without any resource to do anything. Unless the Union itself petitioned that the employer they said was on the -- had to arrive between so (Inaudible) this so -- of violating 8 (a) (2) that is recognizing the Union when indeed it may not represent the majority and for evidence of violating 8 (a) (5) where they did represent the majority.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Mr. Gore --

turns.name: ALBERT GORE
turns.text: To get him -- if I may complete my answer Your Honor. To get him off --

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Are you answering my question?

turns.name: ALBERT GORE
turns.text: Pardon?

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: You are answering my question?

turns.name: ALBERT GORE
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Alright.

turns.name: ALBERT GORE
turns.text: To get him off of that -- to get him off of that Congress gave the employer the right to file the petition, and when that petition is filed, that petition is process by the Board. So, he can have as an election and he can't have a forthwith.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: But in the meantime as I understand you're submission, he is under a duty to bargain?

turns.name: ALBERT GORE
turns.text: Your Honor, at the time that he files his petition, he is not under a duty to bargain.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Even though he had to represent him --

turns.name: ALBERT GORE
turns.text: Because that is the one exception that has been constructed into the Act by Congress.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Well, then I come back with the --

turns.name: ALBERT GORE
turns.text: The only time --

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: I come back to what I said to you earlier?

turns.name: ALBERT GORE
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: As I understand it, what you're telling us is that in your view, the Union comes into the employer and although the employer may have no quarrel with you about the authenticity of signatures, no quarrel with you about the number of cards as representing the majority in the appropriate bargaining unit. Nevertheless, I mean the employers not required to bargain if instead, he files his own petition with the Board requesting an election, is that right?

turns.name: ALBERT GORE
turns.text: That is correct. Now --

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: I don't see -- I just don't see how much difference there is between you and what Mr. Manoli told?

turns.name: ALBERT GORE
turns.text: Oh! The difference is vast. The difference is vast because under the administrative rules of the Board, when the employer files the petition, delays are not available to him like they are when the Union files a petition. He can not -- he comes in and he must state what the appropriate unit is. He must state what his position is in respect to illegibility. He must -- he must take his position that all these methods and at that point the Union can say, “Yes, we agree.” And immediately you have no pass. Whereas, when the employer sits on hands and says, “You file the petition. Whatever you say in your petition we're going to disagree with. We're going to raise an objection be it frivolous or not on the appropriateness of the unit.” You say who are the supervisors will object to who you say the supervisors are. And under those circumstances, under the rules of the Board in a no issue case -- in a no issue case, an employer has at least 45 days in a no issue case to go to an election and in my experience we had have cases with petitions filed that have gone beyond the year from the time of the filing of petition before an election is held.

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: But Mr. Gore let's see, the difference between your position and that of Mr. Manoli. Is that Mr. Manoli say -- you say that after the Union presents the authorization cards to the employer. The employer is under a duty to bargain with the Union unless and until the employer files a claim for hearing.

turns.name: ALBERT GORE
turns.text: Your Honor, that is correct with one clarification.

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: Now wait a minute, is that right?

turns.name: ALBERT GORE
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: Mr. Manoli says that when the Union presents the authorization cards to the employer, if the employer says, I don't believe in cards and I refuse to accept these cards. And does not engage in unfair labor practice then the employer is not, is not under an obligation to bargain with the Union --

turns.name: ALBERT GORE
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: Then there has to be an election presumably on the petition of the Union.

turns.name: ALBERT GORE
turns.text: That is his position, yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: Now, have I stated the difference between the two of it correctly?

turns.name: ALBERT GORE
turns.text: Yes. But the difference as you stated seems small indeed they are -- apart.

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: I'm not arguing whether it's small or not. Is that the difference?

turns.name: ALBERT GORE
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: And you're taking the position that what Mr. Manoli told us is not reflected in the statute or in any Board decisions.

turns.name: ALBERT GORE
turns.text: That is correct.

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: That there are no decisions by the National Labor Relations Board that sets forth with what Mr. Manoli has told us, is that right?

turns.name: ALBERT GORE
turns.text: Yes. I have the red light.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: You may answer fully after this. Mr. Gore, you may continue with your arguments.

turns.name: ALBERT GORE
turns.text: Alright. I'd like to complete my answer to Judge Fortas' question. Perhaps I was a bit presumptuous to suggest what the Board's position is as of today. My -- I have suggested --now, what my views of the Board's decisions might be and what their position has been unquestionably Mr. Manoli is in a better position to suggest what the Board's position is as of today than I. And then, if the presumption that I --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: How about yesterday?

turns.name: ALBERT GORE
turns.text: Yes. Well, I think -- yesterday's position I thought I understood.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Well, do you -- are there any cases that you know of which articulates the view that Mr. Manoli --

turns.name: ALBERT GORE
turns.text: Well, the Aaron Brothers case that Mr. Manoli suggested articulates a view that an employer may refuse to recognize under certain circumstances when there are no unfair labor practices. It also articulates the view that the employer is -- has a duty to bargain impose upon him --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Unless he has a good faith doubt?

turns.name: ALBERT GORE
turns.text: -- even -- yes -- even when there are no unfair labor practices.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: ALBERT GORE
turns.text: The Aaron brothers case does not sustain a -- as I understand it, I should say Your Honor, does not sustain the view that an employer can't -- can refuse to recognized blatantly. The Union which has been designated by the employees merely because he says, he doesn't like cards. I don't think that that suggested in the Aaron Brothers case. Indeed if the Board takes that position, it is my view that this is clearly contrary to the law. Now, I think the mistake that is made is that somewhere along the line, the Board has forgotten that we're talking about employees' rights. This Court suggested that an employer may have a duty to bargain imposed upon him by authorization cards in the Arkansas Flooring case. But when let's farther actually in the International Ladies Garments Workers Union against the NLRB, which I generally refer to as the Bernard Olton case. In that case, there was a questioned violation of Section 8 (a) (2). There the employer had recognized a union on the basis of authorization cards without checking the cards believing that there was a majority. This Court and oh, I should add and there was no question that case. None whatsoever but that the employer was in good faith in recognizing the Union. This Court held that good faith did not satisfy prohibited conduct. This Court said that the employees' rights were paramount. This Court said that the employer has a duty when cards are submitted to it -- to countercheck the cards against payroll.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: You're arguing in no matter what decision of the Board it is wrong.

turns.name: ALBERT GORE
turns.text: That is quite correct.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Well, I should think in view of the ruling in that case, it would -- the employer's alternative duty to ask for an election.

turns.name: ALBERT GORE
turns.text: That is correct. And if it does not it is violated the law.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Well, no. You told us just now that it could alternatively check the cards on its own.

turns.name: ALBERT GORE
turns.text: Yes. Oh, yes. It may check the cards.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: But it doesn't have --

turns.name: ALBERT GORE
turns.text: Ever since the cards and finds that there is a majority that recognizes.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Well, what's --

turns.name: ALBERT GORE
turns.text: If it checks the cards and finds there is no majority, it shall not recognize.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Can he ask employees, did you sign this card?

turns.name: ALBERT GORE
turns.text: If there is a question about the -- if there is a question about the authenticity of the signature. However, I would suggest Your Honor that the employer has within his means sufficient basis to check the authenticity of the signatures without going to the employers.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: With the handwriting against it.

turns.name: ALBERT GORE
turns.text: Where with no W-2 forms and many other things that they have. And if then if there still remains the question, there's no -- he has the right to ask the employees.

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: Mr. Gore, do I understand your position that the when the Union comes in and says, here are the cards. And the employer says, “Thanks, I'm going to ask for an election.” No more. That is if employer is in good shape?

turns.name: ALBERT GORE
turns.text: The employer is in good shape if he does not engage in other conduct which is inconsistent with the free choice of the employees. This was an exception in this multi-facetted Act that was imposed by Mr. (Inaudible) --

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Now, in that respect Mr. Gore, do I understand you correctly. The employer doesn't have to do anything when the Union comes with the cards, if he wants to say, “No, I'm going to file a petition for an election.”

turns.name: ALBERT GORE
turns.text: And does work with fire --

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: ALBERT GORE
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: And if he does that, he doesn't have to check cards. He doesn't have to do anything?

turns.name: ALBERT GORE
turns.text: That is correct.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: He doesn't have to make any comment of any kind.

turns.name: ALBERT GORE
turns.text: That is correct.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: ALBERT GORE
turns.text: If he goes forward and files for an election and under those circumstances, the representation procedures of the NLRB are expedited. He does not have within his power to control delay under those circumstances of free choice of the employees, assuming again that he does not engage inconsistent conduct thereafter.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Is there a time limit for the completion of proceedings under employers' petition?

turns.name: ALBERT GORE
turns.text: No.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Is there a time limit on the completion of proceedings under the Unions' petition?

turns.name: ALBERT GORE
turns.text: In fortiori.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: And I take it that you would say that the employer has the same right of free speech at that time and the pending the election?

turns.name: ALBERT GORE
turns.text: Rights under that 8 (c) are protected at all times. Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: And he could I suppose say to the employees, you may have signed these cards but that you certainly made a mistake and we are going to -- and when you heard the whole facts you'll vote against the Union.

turns.name: ALBERT GORE
turns.text: I don't know that I would accept the statement that you've made a mistake but I think that he would have a right to tell the employees that you have a right to freely to choose in the election. Then you are not required to vote in accordance with the way you signed cards.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: And here's why -- and here's why it would be better to vote against the Union?

turns.name: ALBERT GORE
turns.text: Oh, he would have a right to express his views --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: ALBERT GORE
turns.text: -- again so that they do not contain within the veiled threats or promises of benefit which would affect the employees free choice.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: May I ask you a question to see if I clearly understand --

turns.name: ALBERT GORE
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: -- the difference between you. As I understand, you take the position that when the employees give cards to the employer, he has a right under the law say, “Alright, I don't want to do this. I prefer an election.”

turns.name: ALBERT GORE
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Then he can answer to the election not -- the Union doesn't have to ask for it but he can ask for it. And that after he asked for it, you have an election in which you have the same rights and free speech as both sides as though the Union had asked for an election.

turns.name: ALBERT GORE
turns.text: Correct. Now, I should add that this is been the view of the Fourth Circuit before Logan Packing and it was expressed in the Florence Printing Company case an overnight express.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Well, how may I -- may I ask about what the very difference there is between you and Mr. Manoli about who must initiate this election in the circumstances of Mr. Justice Black's question. As I understood you, you said that it was then the duty of the employer to request election --

turns.name: ALBERT GORE
turns.text: Correct.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: -- and I understood that Mr. Manoli thought it was the employees which should go forward with demanding an election.

turns.name: ALBERT GORE
turns.text: I understand that could be the position of the Board, yes.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: You understand that to be the position --

turns.name: ALBERT GORE
turns.text: Well, of course Mr. Manoli I should say.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: I beg your pardon.

turns.name: ALBERT GORE
turns.text: I understand that is Mr. Manoli's position that there is not a requirement on the employer to file a petition under Section 9 (c) (1) (b) in order to avoid a refusal to recognize.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Well, are there other decisions of the National Labor Relations Board pro or con on that subject in the past.

turns.name: ALBERT GORE
turns.text: This case --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: I beg your pardon.

turns.name: ALBERT GORE
turns.text: This case in Gissel Packing case, the Board accentuated the fact and rely the great measure upon the fact that the employer did not file a petition -- our petition under Section 9 (c) (1) (b). I should however add that this case presents an addition numerous unfair labor practices engaged in by the employer including this charges which were obviously inconsistent with the -- a desire to give the employees a free choice. But in this case, they specifically talked about the failure of the employer to file a 9 (c) (1) (b) petition.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Why did they say it was his duty?

turns.name: ALBERT GORE
turns.text: Pardon?

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Why did they say it was his duty?

turns.name: ALBERT GORE
turns.text: Well, they said --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: ALBERT GORE
turns.text: They said that its failure to do this showed that he was not in good faith. Is the position that the Board took.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Not his failure to file?

turns.name: ALBERT GORE
turns.text: Yes. The failure to file a 9 (c) (1) (b) petition was an act of not in good faith. Now here, of course is where we differ from the Board again and that we don't think that good faith is relevant.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Whether he has a right?

turns.name: ALBERT GORE
turns.text: So the question of a duty to recognize.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: He has a right to file for an election, doesn't he? Which the Board could knock him out?

turns.name: ALBERT GORE
turns.text: That of -- he has the right to file for an election. That is correct.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Either one of them?

turns.name: ALBERT GORE
turns.text: That is correct.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: So why shouldn't he file it if he is not satisfied with the card?

turns.name: ALBERT GORE
turns.text: I think he should but he does not Your Honor and he does not because he gains time by not filing. He gains time to engage in all kinds of conduct that if -- a conduct which can be viewed as being illegal and conduct which we never learned about.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Do you think it's crucial for us to find in this case whether the obligation in those circumstances is on the employer or the Union to file for the election.

turns.name: ALBERT GORE
turns.text: I think it's crucial to find that when the employer does not file such a petition on the mere tender of cards, the majority cards, the employer has violated his duty and has refused to recognize and thereby has violated the employee's rights in designating their bargaining representative.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: And the only way you can relieve himself of that is through demand an election?

turns.name: ALBERT GORE
turns.text: By filing a petition on its own. That is correct.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Yes. Yes, yes that's absolutely correct.

turns.name: ALBERT GORE
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: Mr. Gore --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: You and Mr. Manoli are in distinct disagreement on that are you?

turns.name: ALBERT GORE
turns.text: Apparently.

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: Mr. Gore isn't that possible to dispose of these two cases without reaching most of the questions you've been discussing.

turns.name: ALBERT GORE
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: That is to say, as I understand it that these in both of these cases, the Board has found affirmative acts which the Board says constitute unfair labor practices. Now, it is conceivable I suppose to decide these cases on the basis that where the employer engages in affirmative positive acts of unfair labor practices. Then, the employer is not justified in refusing to proceed to bargain on the basis of the authorization cards?

turns.name: ALBERT GORE
turns.text: That is correct. Indeed the -- it was that aspect of the case that I intended -- originally intended to address my attention to the Court.

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: Or was that aspect of the case that I got out of the briefs and then we heard a lot of things that started of this course.

turns.name: ALBERT GORE
turns.text: Thank you. If you'll excuse me I wish to save some time for rebuttal for issue.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Mr. Jenkins.

turns.name: JOHN E. JENKINS, JR.
turns.text: May it please the Court. Perhaps it would be helpful to the Court having heard this general discussion of the principles that are involved in the issues in this case. If we take Gissel as an example from a factual standpoint and see how the application of these principles came up with one little sausage maker back in the hills of West Virginia. Back in the 1960's about 1961, this union started a massive organization campaign in the retail food industry there and it attempted to organize the Logan Packing Company, the Gissel Packing Company and many other companies. Petitions were filed, cards were taken up and the Union claimed that had a majority of the employees. And in 1961, the National Labor Relations Board came in, pitched its tents in this company premises, in order the employees in to vote without the President of the company hanging over their shoulder or without the Union organizer paying over their and a free expression of their wishes.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: On whose petition was that?

turns.name: JOHN E. JENKINS, JR.
turns.text: Among the petition of the Union.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: In the Union.

turns.name: JOHN E. JENKINS, JR.
turns.text: And when this occurred and the Union having represented as it must have been a necessity that it had a majority because it had demanded recognition before that in these cases. The employees and all of these companies rejected the Union. Three or four years past and the Union was still under great pressure from its clients William P. Krueger and the Big Chains to organize this industry. This was no spontaneous rising with union interest of the employees in this companies and they started in with the new technique which the Board had developed for them that is on trial before your -- this Court today, the authorization card technique. And in January of 1965, they contacted Gissel, my client, and said, “We represent the majority of your employees bargain with us or we'll file unfair labor practices against you. We got some cards.” Now, Gissel people had been down this road before. They knew these cards were unreliable. They had knew the propensities of this union to overstate its claim or its case of representation.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Had there been cards back in 1961 or just claims?

turns.name: JOHN E. JENKINS, JR.
turns.text: There had been cards submitted by the Union to the Board as a prerequisite to getting the election. There had not been authorization cards as I understand it submitted to the company, Your Honor. And so, the company immediately responded to this request and they said this, “We don't have any confidence in these cards.” Secondly, why not let's get this thing settled properly by a fair and honest election, and wrote the Union a letter and asked them to petition for an election. Now, it is an act of complete futility for an employer in these circumstances to file a petition for election. We did it in Sehon Stevenson, it was refused. We did it in Davis Wholesale, it was refused. We did it in Logan Packing Company, it was refused. In Gissel, the company wrote a letter to the Union and invited them to file the petition and --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: And Mr. -- and Mr. Manoli says, that when the employer asked them when they'll -- he'll get it. Did you understand him to say that?

turns.name: JOHN E. JENKINS, JR.
turns.text: I certainly did and its most and like.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: You did ask and you didn't get it?

turns.name: JOHN E. JENKINS, JR.
turns.text: We -- I asked time and again, and we did not get it.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: In this case?

turns.name: JOHN E. JENKINS, JR.
turns.text: No, sir. In this case, we decided to try a different attack we thought maybe the Union has more persuasive authority with the Board than we do. Let's write the Union a letter and suggest a quick consent election and maybe if they petition they can get it because this is what we want. Of course, they didn't want an election because they knew they didn't represent a majority of this people.

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: But if you'd ask an election --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Didn't you file it any how? We can --

turns.name: JOHN E. JENKINS, JR.
turns.text: We thought it was a few --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: It was a question passed on?

turns.name: JOHN E. JENKINS, JR.
turns.text: We feel -- we thought it was futile act Your Honor and in Logan Packing Company, we did file the petition and it was thrown out in Davis Wholesale Company currently in the Court of Appeals for the DC Circuit, we filed it and it was thrown out --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Why was it thrown out?

turns.name: JOHN E. JENKINS, JR.
turns.text: They said it, we're going -- you're going to have to honor those cards. You've got to accept --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Anything more behind it than that, was it?

turns.name: JOHN E. JENKINS, JR.
turns.text: Yes, sir. There were collateral. Unfair labor practices of the company involved of supervisors of the company. Now --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: That might not make it necessarily futile act for you to ask where there were not, would it?

turns.name: JOHN E. JENKINS, JR.
turns.text: I don't know. It is --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: What is -- gets him. Have you been guilty of any unfair labor practice?

turns.name: JOHN E. JENKINS, JR.
turns.text: Pardon sir?

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: What?

turns.name: JOHN E. JENKINS, JR.
turns.text: Pardon sir?

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Had you been guilty of any unfair labor case -- practice in this case?

turns.name: JOHN E. JENKINS, JR.
turns.text: The Board so found. Yes, sir. State coercive statements of some people -- they didn't like the Union expressions of opinion which the Board found were coercive and unfair labor practices. Now --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: When did they found that?

turns.name: JOHN E. JENKINS, JR.
turns.text: They found that as part of this case which is here now in which they found that the company had committed some other peripheral unfair labor practices and that it had a duty to bargain with the Union on basis of these cards alone.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Well, that was on the card issues.

turns.name: JOHN E. JENKINS, JR.
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Did you have fraud on an election before that hearing was held on that unfair labor practice?

turns.name: JOHN E. JENKINS, JR.
turns.text: The -- the hearing Your Honor was all combined in one hearing. The election and card question and the unfair labor practices were all in one hearing. We did not ask for an election. In this case, we wrote a letter to the Union when the demand was originally made we're suggesting that they petition for an election in this case.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: But that was the only way you asked about it?

turns.name: JOHN E. JENKINS, JR.
turns.text: Yes, sir. Because you see Your Honor, we felt and we believe the law so provides. If you read Section 9, it never says in there that the employer must petition.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: But it doesn't say much. You said it can if you want to.

turns.name: JOHN E. JENKINS, JR.
turns.text: It's a right that he has but certainly he's rights in the matter should not be determined until whether he file, initiates a petition of the Union. The Union is the one that is asking for recognition. The Board had uniformly --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Not if there's one declining to give it to him?

turns.name: JOHN E. JENKINS, JR.
turns.text: Pardon sir?

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: The Company is the one that's declining to give it to them.

turns.name: JOHN E. JENKINS, JR.
turns.text: This is true. Then if the -- if the Union then still wants recognition, they have this avenue open to them to go and ask for an election if they want it. We simply suggest it to them in this case if they do this.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: The Board found here did it not that you -- the company had been guilty of unfair labor practices before any presentation of these signatures?

turns.name: JOHN E. JENKINS, JR.
turns.text: Yes, sir. They were on both sides chronologically.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: I beg your pardon?

turns.name: JOHN E. JENKINS, JR.
turns.text: On both sides chronologically. Most of the alleged unfair labor practices occurred subsequent to the dates on this authorization cards.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: They are told that if they got talking to Union men, you blank the blank things will go, didn't they or didn't they find that coerced --

turns.name: JOHN E. JENKINS, JR.
turns.text: Evidence to that effect then that Board accepted it. Yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: That is before the cards were submitted.

turns.name: JOHN E. JENKINS, JR.
turns.text: No, Your Honor. The -- the cards were submitted and then statements were made. I'm not sure in my mind about the chronology of each of these incidents that you're talking about there they were all and relatively the same general period of time there.

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: Did I understand you correctly to say that your client wanted an election?

turns.name: JOHN E. JENKINS, JR.
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: But didn't want to ask for it?

turns.name: JOHN E. JENKINS, JR.
turns.text: No, sir. We wrote a union -- a letter to the Union asking them to ask for it and suggesting it.

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: But you knew you could have petitioned?

turns.name: JOHN E. JENKINS, JR.
turns.text: We thought it was a free --

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: Whether or not the Board granted it, you have a right to petition for it.

turns.name: JOHN E. JENKINS, JR.
turns.text: We have -- we had a right to petition the election. We did it in the companion cases. The Board refused it and so we decided in Gissel to simply suggest to the Union do it.

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: And did you also realize that if you petition and you continue these unfair labor practices, you would be in deeper trouble?

turns.name: JOHN E. JENKINS, JR.
turns.text: We -- I do not think Your Honor that that the two are related to each other. You see, there is a question of who is going to represent the employees. The whole structure of the labor law is to preserve the rights of employees. They are not apprise over which a union and a company, fights. The question of representation of them and there freedom of choice is one thing to be dealt with by the Board and there an employer or union's unfair labor practices are something separate and distinct from that.

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: But I understand that once you ask for the petition for the election, you have all the freedoms of speech and then by all set.

turns.name: JOHN E. JENKINS, JR.
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: Right?

turns.name: JOHN E. JENKINS, JR.
turns.text: That is true. As long as --

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: But you didn't?

turns.name: JOHN E. JENKINS, JR.
turns.text: We -- we don't think --

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: You want -- you didn't petition for your election and you did exercise which you thought was freedom of speech but we're actually unfair labor practice.

turns.name: JOHN E. JENKINS, JR.
turns.text: We hope Your Honor that the right of freedom of speech of any citizen of the country is not contention upon him filing a paper with the National Labor Relations Board. We think he has that right inherently God given whether he files a petition or anything else.

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: Despite what the law said?

turns.name: JOHN E. JENKINS, JR.
turns.text: Pardon sir?

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: Despite what the law says?

turns.name: JOHN E. JENKINS, JR.
turns.text: I do not think the law says --

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: What you really mean is that what you think is freedom of speech is one thing and what they are now law be thinks that freedom of speech is another thing, and what the law says freedom of speech is a very thing.

turns.name: JOHN E. JENKINS, JR.
turns.text: We have our differences of what freedom of speech is Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: And so you choose to take the other side instead of petition.

turns.name: JOHN E. JENKINS, JR.
turns.text: We did not think that the law employers' rights of freedom of speech were conditioned upon him filing a paper of any kind with anybody.

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: But why did you write to file the paper with the Union?

turns.name: JOHN E. JENKINS, JR.
turns.text: We wrote that to try to see if by this means, we could get this question resolved properly and fairly.

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: Well, would it --

turns.name: JOHN E. JENKINS, JR.
turns.text: We --

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: -- promptly, would the Union's petition get faster action in the employers?

turns.name: JOHN E. JENKINS, JR.
turns.text: We had tried the employer petition and failed and so we thought we try the Union side of it.

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: You didn't try it in this case?

turns.name: JOHN E. JENKINS, JR.
turns.text: Pardon sir.

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: How about the other cases? Yes, you didn't try it here.

turns.name: JOHN E. JENKINS, JR.
turns.text: No. This is true.

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: Nice to know what it.

turns.name: JOHN E. JENKINS, JR.
turns.text: This is true. But we don't think Your Honor that an employers' rights of what his conduct is or dependent upon whether he initiates a petition or whether a union initiates a petition and certainly not insofar as rights as fundamental as free speech.

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: Mr. Jenkins, did the Union in these other cases where you did file a petition for an election. Did they -- Board give any reason as to why that was not granted?

turns.name: JOHN E. JENKINS, JR.
turns.text: It's a matter of Board policy and that is the only reason -- it is the matter of Board policy that they want to merge unfair labor practices and representation questions together. Confuse the two and not decide on representation questions until unfair questions are determined. And so if --

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: That sounds to me as if that's very likely what they said to you.

turns.name: JOHN E. JENKINS, JR.
turns.text: No, Your Honor. That is my gloss on what they do.

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: Now, what I like to know is what they say, give you any reason and if so what was that reason? And then you may go ahead of course if you like with your gloss.

turns.name: JOHN E. JENKINS, JR.
turns.text: What they said was they wrote a letter and said since -- let me go back in this case to make it clear again. Of course, we did not file a petition. In Logan we did, so I'll have to answer you in terms of Logan, in Davis, in Sehon, and all the others. In those cases, we filed the petition promptly.
